1: Shimou zot ha-kohanim -- 'Hear this, O priests!' Ch 5 opens with a triple summons: priests, house of Israel, and the royal house are all called to account. Ve-haqshivu beit-Yisrael -- 'Listen, O house of Israel!' Ve-beit ha-melekh ha-azinu -- 'Give ear, O house of the king!' All three leadership institutions -- cultic, national, and royal -- bear responsibility. Ki lakhem ha-mishpat -- 'for the judgment is against you.' Mishpat (H4941, judgment/justice) -- the covenant lawsuit reaches its verdict. Ki pahh hayitem le-Mitzpah -- 'for you have been a snare at Mizpah.' Mizpah and Tabor were cultic and administrative centers; the leaders stationed there to guide Israel have become traps (H6341, pahh) -- snares set for the people rather than guides.
2: Ve-shachata ha-sheetim he-imiku -- 'The rebels are deep in their corruption.' The slaughter pits and the depth of rebellion. Va-anochi musar le-kulam -- 'though I have been a discipline to all of them.' YHWH has provided ongoing correction and instruction -- the leadership has refused to learn from it. The divine pedagogy of history (the plagues, the desert wandering, the judges' cycles) has gone unheeded by those who should have taught its lessons.
3: Ani yadati Efrayim ve-Yisrael lo-nichad mimmenni -- 'I know Ephraim, and Israel is not hidden from me.' YHWH's knowledge (yada) is comprehensive and covenantal -- he knows Ephraim with the intimacy of the covenant partner who sees everything. Ki attah hizne'atah Efrayim -- 'Ephraim, you have now played the whore.' The use of the perfect tense suggests completed, settled adultery. Nitma Yisrael -- 'Israel has become defiled.' Nitma (H2930, to be made unclean/defiled) -- the ritual impurity that results from idolatry. Israel's spiritual adultery has produced a state of defilement comparable to ritual impurity.
4: Lo-yitnu maleleihem la-shuv el-Eloheihem -- 'Their deeds do not permit them to return to their God.' The sin has created its own momentum: the maleleihem (their deeds) actively prevent repentance. Ki ruach zenutim be-qirbam -- 'for a spirit of prostitution is within them.' The ruach zenutim of 4:12 returns: not merely external behavior but an internalized spirit that drives away from YHWH. Ve-et-YHWH lo yadau -- 'and they do not know the LORD.' The failure of knowledge (daath) appears again as the consequence and cause simultaneously: they do not know YHWH, so the spirit of prostitution fills the vacuum; the spirit of prostitution prevents them from knowing YHWH. The cycle is self-reinforcing.
5: Ve-anah gaon Yisrael be-fanav -- 'Israel's arrogance testifies against him to his face.' Gaon (H1347, pride/arrogance/majesty) -- the pride that should have been directed toward YHWH (his glory/majesty) has been redirected to Israel itself. The arrogance becomes self-accusing: ve-Yisrael ve-Efrayim yikkashelu ba-avonam -- 'Israel and Ephraim will stumble in their iniquity.' Ve-kashhal gam-Yehudah immam -- 'and Judah will stumble with them.' The judgment expands: Judah's warning (Hosea 4:15) has not been heeded, and Judah too will share in the northern kingdom's fate.
6: Be-tzonam u-veqqaram yelekhu le-vaqesh et-YHWH ve-lo yimtzau -- 'They will go with their flocks and herds to seek the LORD, but they will not find him.' The seeking of YHWH with cultic offerings (flocks and herds) without covenant heart-knowledge produces religious activity with no access to God. Chalatz mehehem -- 'He has withdrawn from them.' Chalatz (H2502, to withdraw/draw out) -- YHWH removes himself from the cultic encounter. The sacrifices continue; YHWH is absent. This is the prophetic critique of ex opere operato religion: ritual performance without covenant relationship cannot summon the divine presence.
7: Be-YHWH bagadu ki-vanim zarim yaladu -- 'They have been unfaithful to the LORD, because they have borne illegitimate children.' Bagad (H898, to deal treacherously/be unfaithful) -- the same verb used for Gomer's treachery (Hosea 2:2). The 'illegitimate children' (banim zarim) are those born of the religious syncretism: the mixed offspring of YHWH-worship and Baal-worship. Attah yokhal otam yareach et-chelqehem -- 'Now the new moon will devour them along with their fields.' The new moon festival (chodesh, cf. Hosea 2:11) that was suspended as covenant judgment now becomes the instrument of devouring -- the sacred time becomes the time of destruction.
8: Tiqu shofar be-Giveah chatzotzera ba-Ramah -- 'Sound the ram's horn in Gibeah, the trumpet in Ramah.' The war alarm -- shofar (H7782, ram's horn) and chatzotzera (H2689, trumpet) -- signals imminent Assyrian invasion. Gibeah and Ramah are towns on the southern border of the northern kingdom (or the northern border of Benjamin), the path of invasion. Hariu Beit-Aven acharekha Binyamin -- 'raise the battle cry at Beth-aven: Behind you, Benjamin!' The invasion sweeps from north to south; Benjamin (in Judah's territory, south of Ephraim) is warned that the army is behind them. The cult site of Beth-aven (the renamed Bethel, Hosea 4:15) will not protect Israel when Assyria comes.
9: Efrayim le-shammah tihyeh be-yom tokhechah -- 'Ephraim will be laid waste on the day of punishment.' Shammah (H8047, desolation/horror) -- the total devastation of the northern kingdom. Ba-shivtei Yisrael hodati nemannah -- 'among the tribes of Israel I have declared what is certain.' YHWH's word is reliable (nemannah, H539, trustworthy/faithful): the coming judgment is certain because it rests on YHWH's covenant word.
10: Hayu sarei Yehudah ke-massigei gevul -- 'The princes of Judah have become like those who move boundary stones.' The boundary stone (gevul, H1366) was protected by covenant law (Deuteronomy 19:14; Hosea 27:17); moving it was a form of fraud and theft. Judah's princes are using their authority to expand their own territory at others' expense -- the social-justice critique applied to the southern kingdom. Aleihem eshpokh ka-mayim evrati -- 'I will pour out my wrath on them like water.' The wrath poured like water is catastrophic and all-encompassing -- not a measured response but an overwhelming flood.
11: Ashuq Efrayim rashatz ba-mishpat -- 'Ephraim is oppressed and crushed in justice.' Ashaq (H6231, to oppress/wrong) and ratzatz (H7533, to crush/shatter) -- double verbs of violence applied to the judgment of Ephraim. Ki hoel halakh acharei tzav -- 'because he was determined to pursue worthless idols.' Tzav (H6673) is debated: it may mean 'filth/dung' (a contemptuous word for idols), 'human command' (following human rather than divine authority), or 'empty things.' The MKT reads 'worthless idols.' Ephraim's determined pursuit of idolatry has resulted in the crushing oppression that his covenant faithfulness would have prevented.
12: Ve-anokhi ka-ash le-Efrayim -- 'I will be like a moth to Ephraim.' The divine judgment imagery shifts from violent (lion, Hosea 5:14) to slow and insidious: a moth (ash, H6211) eating fabric -- invisible, gradual, but ultimately destructive. Ve-ka-raqav le-veit Yehudah -- 'and like rot to the house of Judah.' Raqav (H7538, rot/dry rot) -- the slow decay that hollows out a structure before it collapses. The judgment of both kingdoms is described as slow decay rather than sudden catastrophe -- the long decline of covenant faithlessness before the Assyrian blow.
13: Va-yar Efrayim et-cholyo -- 'When Ephraim saw his sickness.' The political response to the covenant decay is to seek foreign alliance rather than divine healing. Ve-Yehudah et-mazzo -- 'and Judah saw his wound.' Va-yelekh Efrayim el-Ashshur -- 'Ephraim turned to Assyria.' The northern kingdom's appeal to Assyria for political security is documented historically (2 Kings 15:19; Hosea 17:3-4). Ve-yishlach el-melek yarev -- 'and sent to the great king.' Melekh yarev (the great/warring king) -- an epithet for the Assyrian king; possibly a transliteration of the Assyrian royal title. Ve-hu lo yukhal lirpa lakhem -- 'but he is not able to heal you.' The Assyrian king cannot cure what only YHWH can heal: the covenant sickness requires the covenant physician.
14: Ki anokhi ka-shachal le-Efrayim -- 'For I will be like a lion to Ephraim.' The image shifts from moth (Hosea 5:12) to lion (shachal, H7826, fierce/great lion): slow decay gives way to sudden violent destruction. Ve-ka-kefir le-veit Yehudah -- 'like a young lion to the house of Judah.' Kefir (H3715, young lion/growing lion) -- the energy and power of a young predator. Ani ani etaref ve-eelekh essa ve-ein matzil -- 'I myself will tear them apart and go away; I will carry off and no one will rescue.' The divine withdrawal after judgment: YHWH attacks, tears, carries off, and departs -- leaving the wounded prey with no rescuer. The same YHWH who refused to be found when sought with flocks (Hosea 5:6) now actively pursues in judgment.
15: Elekh ashuva el-meqomi -- 'I will return to my place.' YHWH withdraws to the divine dwelling (his 'place') after the judgment -- the same hidden God of Isaiah 45:15. Ad asher-yeshlemu ve-viqshu fanai -- 'until they acknowledge their guilt and seek my face.' The withdrawal is purposive: it creates the conditions for genuine repentance. Yeshlemu (H816, to be guilty/suffer punishment) -- acknowledging guilt is the first step of return. Be-tzar lahem yeshachshuni -- 'in their distress they will earnestly seek me.' The distress of Assyrian conquest will drive what prosperity and security could not: genuine seeking of YHWH. The hiddenness of God creates the longing that the divine presence does not.
